# Lotus failure — scope v2 §1 ignored research-lead's finding

## What happened
Scope v2 §1 (atom 019de4ad-7822) declared: "Drop .pretty parsing. The first `user` message in the `.jsonl` IS the task." This was wrong on inspection — the first `user` event in real streams contains `tool_result`, not the task prompt.

## What lotus did wrong (gate 0)
Research-lead's stream-json schema atom (019de4a3-77bf) explicitly stated: "Companion pretty: /tmp/launchagent-<sid>.pretty (task description lives here, NOT in JSONL)." Lotus had this finding in hand BEFORE filing scope v2 and ignored it.

The fix proposed in scope v2 §1 was reactive to devil's misread #1 (.pretty parsing fragility). Lotus over-rotated: instead of fixing parsing fragility, redirected to a non-existent data path. Should have proposed the actual fix used in v3 — sidecar `.task` file emitted by launchagent-tmux.

## Trigger of the catch
PdM Q1 (atom 019de4b3-15a2). PdM read scope v2 alongside research-lead's schema atom and noticed the contradiction. Without PdM's halt-and-flag, Banshee would have been built against a phantom data source.

## Pattern (cumulative for the Banshee retro)
This is the third lotus failure in the Banshee spin (after 019de40c-* atom-hygiene-without-scan, 019de410 cmux-without-defect, 019de415 stop-handler-without-source-read). Same shape: lotus accepts a framing without verifying primary source.

In this case, lotus had research-lead's correct finding and chose to override it with a guess at devil resolution time. Devil critique should never override fact atoms; it should drive lotus to find a fix that respects them.

## Refined principle (additive)
When devil critiques and research-lead facts contradict, lotus does not pick one — lotus finds a fix that respects the fact and addresses the critique. If lotus cannot, halt and reask. Do not ship a scope that contradicts a confirmed fact atom.

## Correction
Scope v3 (atom forthcoming) supersedes v2 §1. Sidecar `.task` file written by launchagent-tmux. Edited launchagent-tmux 2026-05-01 — backward compatible, three-line change.

# Devil critique — five misreadings of lotus's session-2026-05-02 self-diagnosis

Source: `lotus-failure-ceo-did-the-work` (019de9cb), transcript signals, and atom corpus.

## Misreading 1 — diagnosed behavior, not causal mechanism

Atom 019de9cb says lotus "skipped chief-of-staff routing entirely." True, but this misnames the failure. The failure is gate 0 wasn't triggered at all — not that the wrong route was taken. Lotus never answered "which deputy owns this?" because the gate-0 self-check wasn't loaded. Filing a dispatch reminder doesn't fix a missing trigger. The trigger is absent; the route table is irrelevant without it.

## Misreading 2 — outcome quality launders process failure

Atom 019de9cb: "Code shipped is clean (322 tests pass, fmt+clippy green) — do not revert." This is survivorship bias. Using output quality to justify not reverting is anti-rationalization per `lotus-anti-rationalization` (019de40b-b09a). The 322 tests are the artifact of lotus doing the work — they don't validate the method. The atom smuggles "it worked" into the correction section, implicitly endorsing hands-on execution when results are clean.

## Misreading 3 — user-complaint treated as acceptable detection

Atom 019de9cb frames the catch as "Trigger of catch: User direct." But `lotus-chief-of-staff` (019de3ac) and `lotus-pipeline` (019de3ae) both define internal gate checks. An external user complaint is not a detection mechanism — it is evidence that all internal detection failed. The atom records "user caught it" without naming the gap: zero internal circuit breaker exists for lotus doing hands-on work.

## Misreading 4 — new principle duplicates existing atoms without superseding them

The "Refined principle" section of 019de9cb adds: "Before lotus picks up an editor or runs a build command, lotus answers in-prompt: 'Which deputy owns this gate?'" This is structurally identical to `lotus-chief-of-staff` (019de3ac) and gate 0 of `lotus-pipeline` (019de3ae). The atom doesn't cite these, doesn't supersede them, doesn't explain why prior atoms failed to prevent recurrence. Filing a duplicate principle without a `supersedes` link is noise, not signal.

## Misreading 5 — pattern acknowledged, learning loop ignored

Atom 019de9cb correctly cites three prior identical failures: stop-handler, cmux adoption, lotus-pickup violation. But asks zero second-order questions: if the same failure shape recurred 3+ times after atoms were filed for each, why would filing this atom produce different behavior? The atom treats atom-filing as the fix mechanism while simultaneously demonstrating that atom-filing didn't fix the prior instances.

# Devil critique — two items lotus avoided naming in session-2026-05-02

Source: `lotus-failure-ceo-did-the-work` (019de9cb), `lotus-anti-rationalization` (019de40b-b09a).

## Missing item 1 — the work succeeded *because* of the role failure, not despite it

Atom 019de9cb notes the output is clean: 322 tests, fmt, clippy. It does not name why. Lotus executed the whole task in a single context: code read, schema designed, db.rs + display.rs + main.rs written, tests fixed, clippy satisfied — no handoff, no serialization loss, no inter-agent translation overhead. The multi-agent pipeline (pm → orchestrator → worker → code-review → verifier) introduces real costs: launchagent spin-up latency, context truncation at each boundary, coordination round-trips. There is zero evidence in any atom that the pipeline would have produced an equivalent or better result for this task. Lotus skipped naming this because naming it undermines the delegation principle.

## Missing item 2 — the only circuit breaker is the user, and the atom does nothing to change that

The catch mechanism for this failure was: user noticed, user called it out, session ended with failure atom filed. No internal mechanism detected that lotus was running `cargo build`, editing Rust files, or running tests directly. Nothing in `lotus-chief-of-staff` (019de3ac), `lotus-pipeline` (019de3ae), or any gate-0 procedure fires when lotus uses Edit/Write/Bash(cargo). The failure atom commits to "going forward" behavior but installs no new detection. The next identical failure will also be caught by the user — or not at all.
